g.data-narration: Welcome to this lesson on Agility and the Cost of Change in software engineering.
g.text: Agility And The Cost Of Change

g.data-narration: The cost of change represents the expense of modifying software, which increases as projects progress through development phases.
g.text: The expense of modifying a software system.
ability to respond to evolving needs.

g.data-narration: Traditional Waterfall treats requirements as fixed, causing costs to spike. Agile embraces change to keep costs relatively flat.
In traditional development, early phase changes are cheap. Later changes require modifying code, tests, and documentation causing ripple effects.
g.text: The Traditional Cost of Change Curve
Requirements Phase: Changes are inexpensive - no code written yet.
Design/Implementation: Changes ripple through multiple components.
Testing/Maintenance: Changes most expensive - extensive rework needed.

g.data-narration: Watch the exponential curve - costs remain low initially then skyrocket as the project nears completion.
Agile keeps costs nearly flat through iterative feedback loops and embracing changes throughout the development lifecycle.
Agile achieves flat costs through iterative development, continuous feedback, and responding to change rather than following a rigid plan.
g.text: • Iterative Development: Work in small increments, delivering working software frequently.
• Continuous Feedback: Regular stakeholder feedback catches changes early.
• Embrace Change: Changes welcomed even late in development - welcome requirements changes.
• Working Software: Primary measure of progress - less documentation overhead.

g.data-narration: The Agile Manifesto emphasizes individuals over processes, working software over documentation, and responding to change over following plans.
g.text: 1. Customer satisfaction through early and continuous delivery of valuable software.
2. Welcome changing requirements, even late in development - harness change for competitive advantage.
3. Deliver working software frequently, from weeks to months with preference to shorter timescale.
4. Business people and developers must work together daily throughout the project.

g.data-narration: Scrum is the most popular Agile framework using sprints, daily standups, and retrospectives to manage iterative development.
Sprint Planning defines what can be delivered and how work will be achieved, setting clear goals for the sprint.
The Daily Standup is a 15-minute meeting where team members share progress, plans, and blockers.
g.text: A 15-minute time-boxed event for the Scrum Team to synchronize activities.

g.data-narration: The Sprint Review demonstrates completed work to stakeholders and gathers feedback for future iterations.
Scrum defines three key roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, and the Development Team, each with distinct responsibilities.
Scrum uses three artifacts: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, and Increment, each with a defined commitment.
Agile methodologies transform software development by embracing change, keeping costs predictable through iterative delivery and continuous feedback.
g.text: Summary: Agility Keeps Cost of Change Flat
